• USNH is 'exploring AI capabilities for supplier risk visibility and procurement intake,' with the compelling event framed as 'Exploring AI capabilities to enhance procurement.'
• Merlin Intake is scoped and the buyer currently runs Jaggaer and Workday, positioning Zycus AI as an additive layer.
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is not set and the step is sending Showpad material for a discovery call, so AI interest is genuine but still early.

sources: SF Products__c: Merlin Intake (product scope recorded, but no discovery context)
SF Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c, Compelling_Event__c: fields unread or null (SOQL Q3 returned no values)
Zero Avoma discovery calls (opp+account discovery returned no matched meetings)
Zero tasks/events with 'pain', 'metrics', 'ROI', or 'business case' in subject or description (SOQL Task/Event scan)
metrics.narrative: No quantified business problem or financial impact has been captured. We do not know what operational pain is driving this procurement, what metrics UNH uses to evaluate intake process performance (cycle time, requisition volume, compliance gaps, user satisfaction), or what ROI/savings case would justify a 75K investment. The only clue is the product scope (Merlin Intake, an AI-powered intake and requisition orchestration tool), which implies pain in manual requisition triage, intake routing delays, or poor user experience in the current P2P front-end. But this is inference from the product, not a buyer-stated pain. In a university procurement environment, the buyer may be optimizing for compliance, cost reduction, or faculty/researcher satisfaction, and we do not know which. Without a discovery call, we cannot confirm the pain, quantify the impact, or tailor the value narrative. The gap is total. The immediate move is to secure a discovery call with Jesse Frink (and ideally the budget owner or process owner above him) and anchor the conversation on their current-state pain and the business case for change.

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing, Evaluator (not Champion, not Executive Sponsor, no IsPrimary flag, no last-contact date)
SF X10b_Champion_Business_Buyer__c, Champion_Identified__c: unread or null (SOQL Q3)
Zero Avoma calls (no evidence of Jesse or anyone advocating, making introductions, or sharing internal intel)
Zero tasks/events showing advocacy behavior (introductions, internal docs shared, competitive pushback in our favor)
SF Next_Step__c: rep-side send with no buyer reply or scheduled call (no engagement signal from Jesse)
champion.narrative: No champion is identified. We have one contact, Jesse Frink (Director of Contracts and Sourcing, Evaluator role), but zero evidence of advocacy, access, or engagement. We do not know if Jesse is a coach, a neutral evaluator, or an administrative gatekeeper. We have not spoken to him, he has not introduced us to anyone, he has not shared internal priorities or the competitive landscape beyond what is logged in Salesforce, and he has not responded to the rep's offer to send Showpad material and schedule a discovery call. The contact role is 'Evaluator', not Champion or Executive Sponsor, which suggests he is running a process, not advocating for Zycus. In a cold RFP scenario, a procurement coordinator like Jesse is often neutral or even adversarial (focused on price and compliance, not on our differentiation). We have no champion, no coach, and no internal advocate. The gap is fatal in a competitive deal. The immediate move is to use the discovery call with Jesse to assess his stance (neutral vs supportive) and to ask for introductions to the business owner, the pain owner, and the budget owner. A deal with three competitors, no discovery call, and no champion is a deal we are not positioned to win. If Jesse is unwilling to open doors, we must find another entry point (VP of Finance, CIO, VP of Operations) or disqualify.

sources: SF Competitors__c: Coupa;GEP Worldwide;JAGGAER (logged 2026-06-25, same day as deal creation)

SF How_are_you_addressing_your_problem_toda__c, Existing_vendor__c, Replacing_What__c, Why_not_Zycus__c, Zycus_Differentiation_Why_Zycus__c: unread or null (SOQL Q3, no current-state or differentiation discovery)
Zero Avoma calls (no competitive conversation, no discovery of incumbent, no positioning of Merlin wedge)
Zero tasks/events with 'Coupa', 'GEP', 'JAGGAER', 'competitive', or 'differentiation' in subject/description beyond the Salesforce log
competition.narrative: We are competing against three major players: Coupa, GEP Worldwide, and JAGGAER, all logged in Salesforce Competitors__c on deal creation (2026-06-25). These are formidable: Coupa is the market leader in cloud P2P with deep university penetration, strong branding, and a full-suite offering that bundles intake, sourcing, contracts, procurement, invoicing, and expenses. GEP is a strong challenger with a unified Source-to-Pay suite and a reputation for enterprise-grade configurability and services-led implementations. JAGGAER (formerly SciQuest) has deep roots in higher education and public sector, with a legacy indirect procurement platform and strong university references. All three offer intake as part of a broader platform, which creates a bundling and integration advantage (one vendor, one contract, one throat to choke). Our wedge is Merlin Intake: a modern, AI-native, best-of-breed intake and orchestration layer purpose-built for intelligent requisition triage, categorization, and routing, with agentic automation that legacy P2P front-ends cannot match. The Merlin positioning is: 'You can bolt our AI intake layer onto your existing ERP or P2P system and get the user experience and intelligence you need without ripping and replacing your entire stack. We integrate, we don't displace.' But we have not yet delivered this narrative to the buyer, we do not know if they value best-of-breed AI or prefer a single bundled vendor, and we do not know if any of the three competitors is an incumbent at UNH (which would be a massive advantage for them). The competitive picture is clear on paper but unexplored in practice. The discovery call must extract: 'Which of these three vendors are you already working with? What is your current P2P and ERP stack? Are you looking for a full-suite replacement or a best-of-breed intake layer?' Until we know the incumbent and the buyer's philosophy (bundled vs best-of-breed), we cannot sharpen the wedge.

sources: SF Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c, Compelling_Event__c, What_if_this_is_not_done__c: unread or null (SOQL Q3)
SF Products__c: Merlin Intake (product scope implies intake/requisition pain, but inference not confirmation)
Zero Avoma calls, zero tasks/events with 'pain', 'bottleneck', 'frustration', 'manual', or 'cycle time' in subject/description
identify_pain.narrative: The specific operational pain UNH is trying to solve is unknown. The product scope (Merlin Intake) implies pain in the intake and requisition submission process: likely slow or manual triage of purchase requests, poor user experience for faculty and staff submitting requisitions, lack of intelligent routing and categorization, or downstream delays because intake is a bottleneck in the P2P workflow. These are reasonable inferences for why a buyer would consider an AI-powered intake tool. But we have not heard the buyer articulate the pain in their own words, we do not know the scale (how many requisitions per month, how many users, how many intake staff), we do not know the cost of the current pain (cycle time, error rate, user complaints, compliance issues), and we do not know who owns the pain (is it the procurement team, the finance team, the faculty/department administrators, or the end-user community?). In a university environment, pain often sits with decentralized department admins and faculty researchers who are frustrated by clunky procurement systems, but the buyer (procurement) may be more focused on compliance and cost control than user satisfaction. We do not know which pain is driving this deal. Without a discovery call, the pain is inferred, not confirmed. The immediate move is to anchor the first call on current-state pain: 'Walk me through how requisitions and intake work today. What's working, what's broken, and what would success look like six months after go-live?'

sources: SF Current_Contract_Expiration__c: unread or null (SOQL Q2)
SF What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c: unread or null (no contracting/legal process detail)
Zero Avoma calls, zero tasks/events with 'MSA', 'SOW', 'legal', 'InfoSec', 'contracting', or 'procurement' in subject/description
Zero OpportunityContactRole entries with Legal, InfoSec, or Procurement VP/Director titles beyond Jesse Frink
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are entirely unknown. We do not know if UNH requires an MSA, whether they use a standard university terms template, whether InfoSec or IT architecture review is required, whether legal redlines are handled by university counsel or the state attorney general's office (common in public institutions), or whether procurement flows through a central shared-services organization for the University System of New Hampshire (USNH, the multi-campus system). We do not know the current contract expiration date for any incumbent vendor (Current_Contract_Expiration__c is unread), which would be a forcing function if an incumbent is up for renewal. We do not know if an SI or implementation partner is involved, or if UNH expects a turnkey implementation, training, and change-management package as part of the 75K. Public universities often have rigid procurement rules (lowest qualified bidder, in-state vendor preference, multi-approval sign-off) that can add 30-60 days to the paper process. We have not mapped any of this. The gap blocks our ability to de-risk the final 60 days of the cycle. The discovery call must extract: 'Walk me through what happens after we agree on commercials. What does your contracting process look like? Who reviews the MSA and SOW? Is there an InfoSec or IT review? How long does legal typically take?'

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing, Evaluator (not EB/Executive Sponsor)
SF X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c: unread or null (SOQL Q3)
Zero Avoma calls, zero tasks/events referencing budget authority, CFO, VP Finance, or executive sponsor
economic_buyer.narrative: The Economic Buyer is unidentified. We have one contact, Jesse Frink (Director of Contracts and Sourcing), whose title suggests a procurement coordinator or sourcing lead, not a budget owner. In a public university system, a 75K software purchase will require approval from a VP of Finance, CFO, VP of Operations, or Provost-level sponsor, depending on whether this is categorized as IT, procurement transformation, or academic operations. We have not engaged anyone at that level, and Jesse's role as 'Evaluator' (not Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor in the contact-role picklist) confirms he is not the decision-maker on budget. The gap is compounded by zero discovery: we do not know the approval chain, whether budget is already allocated, or who the executive sponsor is. In public-sector deals, the EB is often invisible until commercials, but we are 232 days from close and have not yet scheduled the discovery call that would map the org chart. The immediate move is to ask Jesse directly in the first call: 'Who owns the budget for this initiative, and who will need to sign off on a 75K spend?' Until we identify and engage that person, this deal cannot progress past paper-shuffling.

SF Next_Step__c: rep-side send with no buyer-committed milestone ('Justin to send showpad material in preparation for a disco call') implies no scheduled process event
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is unknown. We do not know if this is an open RFP, a sole-source negotiation, or an internal business-case approval. We do not know the timeline (RFI due date, RFP response deadline, vendor demo schedule, shortlist notification, final decision date), the approval steps (IT review, legal review, procurement committee, board sign-off), or who is driving the process internally. The Feb 2027 close date (232 days out) suggests a long public-sector cycle, which is consistent with an RFP process that might include: RFP release (possibly already happened), vendor Q&A period, proposal submission (60-90 days), demos and shortlisting (90-120 days), finalist presentations and reference checks (120-180 days), contract negotiation (180-210 days), and legal/procurement sign-off (210-232 days). But this is speculation. The only process signal we have is the presence of three competitors and a single procurement contact, which suggests a competitive tender, not a sole-source deal. We do not know if we missed the RFP release, if we are being invited to respond, or if Jesse is running a quiet multi-vendor comparison outside a formal RFP. The gap blocks our ability to plan. The first call must extract: 'What does the evaluation process look like? Is there a formal RFP, and if so, when is it due? What are the key milestones between now and February? Who else is involved in the decision internally?'

sources: SF Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c, Shoe_Fit_Criteria_Met__c: unread or null (SOQL Q3)
SF Competitors__c lists Coupa, GEP Worldwide, JAGGAER (multi-vendor context) but no discovery of why these three, what they offer, or how we compare
Zero Avoma calls, zero tasks/events with 'RFP', 'criteria', 'requirements', 'scoring', or 'evaluation' in subject/description
decision_criteria.narrative: We do not know the evaluation criteria. The presence of three competitors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER) and the Qualified stage label suggest this may be an RFP or multi-vendor evaluation, but we have no RFP document, no scoring rubric, no stated priorities (e.g. AI/ML capability, integration with existing ERP, ease of use, cost, implementation speed, vendor stability). Universities typically evaluate on total cost of ownership, integration with student/finance systems (Workday, Banner, PeopleSoft, Oracle), compliance/audit trail, and ease of adoption by faculty and staff. We do not know which of these matter most to UNH, whether AI capability is a differentiator or a nice-to-have, or how Merlin Intake's standalone positioning compares to the bundled P2P suites offered by the three competitors. The product scope (Merlin Intake) is narrow and modern, which could be a wedge if the buyer values best-of-breed AI over a legacy bundled approach, but we have not yet discovered their philosophy. The gap is total and blocks our ability to position. The first discovery call must extract: (1) the formal RFP criteria if an RFP exists, (2) the must-haves vs nice-to-haves, (3) how they weight cost vs capability vs integration complexity, and (4) whether AI-powered intake is a board-level priority or a procurement-led efficiency play.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AI fit signal is available. AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, and AIS_Why__c are unread or null (SOQL Q2), and we have no discovery call to assess the buyer's AI appetite, AI maturity, or AI priorities. The product scope (Merlin Intake, an AI-powered intake tool) suggests we are positioning AI capability, but we do not know if the buyer is AI Hungry (seeking AI as a strategic differentiator), AI Curious (open to AI but not prioritizing it), or AI Resistant (skeptical, risk-averse, or indifferent to AI). Universities can be anywhere on this spectrum: some are AI-forward (using AI for student services, research, and operations), others are cautious (focused on cost and compliance, not innovation). We must discover their AI stance in the first call. If they are AI Hungry or Curious, Merlin's AI-native positioning is the wedge; if Resistant, we pivot to user experience, speed, and cost.

deal_movement.summary: Opportunity created and marked Qualified on 2026-06-25 (five days ago) with no subsequent activity. The only recorded next step is rep-side: 'Justin to send showpad material in preparation for a disco call and eventual walkthrough of merlin intake' (SF Next_Step__c, last modified 2026-06-29). No Salesforce field-history moves, no stage progression, no tasks or events logged, and no Avoma calls discovered. This deal has not moved since creation. The Qualified stage was applied on day one without evidence of discovery, which is a data-quality or process issue (a deal should not be marked Qualified until discovery and need-confirmation have occurred). The deal is static and dark.

items.name: Jesse Frink
items.risk: Single point of contact with no evidence of engagement or advocacy. Title suggests procurement coordinator role (running a process, not a pain owner or budget owner). Evaluator role in OpportunityContactRole (not Champion, not EB, not DM) confirms he is not the decision-maker. We have not yet spoken to Jesse, so we do not know his stance (neutral vs supportive), his access to the EB/DM, or his willingness to open doors. Risk: if Jesse is simply administering an RFP and we cannot get introductions to the business owner and budget authority, we are locked into a single-threaded, price-driven process with no champion and no wedge. The discovery call must assess Jesse's stance and secure multi-threading.
items.title: Director of Contracts and Sourcing

items.detail: RFP trap risk: Deal was qualified without buyer engagement, suggesting we were added to a multi-vendor RFP as a courtesy or compliance inclusion. With three major competitors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER), no champion, no EB relationship, and no discovery call, we may be fulfilling a 'three-bid requirement' with no real chance to win. The next 14 days will determine if this is a genuine opportunity or a paper exercise. If we cannot secure a discovery call, multi-thread to the business owner, and identify a champion or coach, we should disqualify rather than waste cycles on an unwinnable RFP.

Public-sector pricing pressure: Universities are highly price-sensitive and often select the lowest-cost qualified bidder in RFP processes. Coupa, GEP, and JAGGAER all offer bundled P2P suites that may appear more cost-effective than a point-solution Merlin Intake license (75K for intake-only vs a full-suite contract that includes intake, sourcing, contracts, procurement, and invoicing). We have not yet discovered the budget, procurement constraints, or pricing expectations. Risk that 75K amount is aspirational or that the buyer will negotiate down aggressively. We must establish the business case and quantified ROI early (discovery call) and position Merlin Intake as lower total cost of ownership than a full-suite rip-and-replace, not just lower unit price.

Incumbent advantage: If any of the three competitors (especially Coupa or JAGGAER) is already deployed at UNH or within the University System of New Hampshire for other procurement modules (sourcing, contracts, invoicing, expenses), they will have a massive incumbent advantage due to integration, training, vendor relationship inertia, and organizational familiarity. We do not know the current-state tech stack (ERP: Workday, Oracle, PeopleSoft? P2P: Coupa, JAGGAER, Ariba, GEP, homegrown?) or whether any competitor is the incumbent. This is a critical discovery question. If an incumbent exists, we are fighting inertia and references, and our wedge must be pain-based (their intake layer is broken, users hate it, it is holding them back) plus AI differentiation. If no incumbent, we have a cleaner fight, but we must still prove integration simplicity.

No executive sponsor or budget authority identified: In a 75K public-sector deal, approval will require a VP-level or C-level sponsor (VP Finance, CFO, Provost, CIO). We have one contact (Jesse Frink, procurement coordinator) with no access to or engagement with the budget owner. Without identifying and engaging the Economic Buyer, we cannot confirm budget availability, political support, or organizational priority for this initiative. Single-threading through procurement is a recipe for stalling at the commercials stage. The discovery call must map the org chart and secure an introduction to the budget owner within the first 30 days.

champion_strength.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Jesse Frink, Evaluator, not Champion), SF Champion_Identified__c unread/null, zero Avoma calls, zero evidence of advocacy
champion_strength.summary: No champion exists. Jesse Frink is our only contact, and his role is Evaluator (not Champion), his title is procurement-focused (not pain-owner or business-owner), and we have zero evidence of advocacy or engagement. A deal with three competitors, no discovery call, and no champion is a deal we are not positioned to win. Champion development is the single highest priority for the next 14 days. If Jesse is willing to advocate and open doors, we develop him; if not, we must find another entry point (VP Finance, CIO, VP Operations, faculty pain-owner) or disqualify.

items.commitment: Justin to send Showpad material in preparation for a discovery call and eventual walkthrough of Merlin Intake

items.action: Secure a live discovery call with Jesse Frink within 7 days. This is the single highest-priority move. The call objectives are: (1) confirm the buyer's pain and business case for Merlin Intake, (2) discover the current-state tech stack and whether any competitor is an incumbent, (3) extract the decision process, RFP timeline (if an RFP exists), and evaluation criteria, (4) map the org chart and identify the Economic Buyer / budget authority, (5) assess Jesse's stance (neutral evaluator vs potential coach) and ask for introductions to the business owner and pain owner. If Jesse is unwilling to engage or open doors, escalate to find another entry point (VP Finance, CIO, VP Operations) or disqualify. Without this call, the deal is unwinnable.
items.trigger: Deal created 2026-06-25, marked Qualified with no discovery, zero buyer engagement in five days, rep offered Showpad material 2026-06-29 but no call scheduled
items.expected_effect: Discovery call is the gate to everything else: confirms qualification, uncovers the competitive context, maps the stakeholders, and identifies the champion/EB. Without it, the deal remains dark and unwinnable. If successful, this call will either validate the opportunity (we have a real shot, here is the wedge) or expose it as an RFP trap (disqualify and move on). Expected effect: move from the relevant stakeholder / dark to the relevant stakeholder / engaged, unlock multi-threading, and establish the Merlin Intake AI wedge.

items.action: Multi-thread from Jesse Frink (procurement coordinator) to the business owner and budget authority within 14 days of the discovery call. Ask Jesse directly: 'Who owns the intake process and feels the pain day-to-day? Who controls the budget for this initiative? Can you introduce me to them?' The target roles are: VP of Finance, VP of Operations, CIO, or a senior procurement/finance leader above Jesse. A 75K deal in a public university requires VP or C-level sign-off. If we remain single-threaded through Jesse, we will stall at commercials or lose on price. Multi-threading is non-negotiable in a competitive deal.
items.trigger: Single contact (Jesse Frink, Evaluator, procurement coordinator) with no EB, no DM, no champion, no business owner engagement. SF OpportunityContactRole shows one entry; zero Avoma calls.
items.expected_effect: Multi-threading breaks us out of the procurement silo and gets us in front of the budget owner and pain owner. This is critical for three reasons: (1) confirms budget and political support, (2) allows us to discover and shape the business case (not just respond to an RFP), (3) creates the opportunity to identify and develop a champion. Expected effect: move from single-threaded / high-risk to multi-threaded / defensible, map the Economic Buyer, and establish a relationship with a potential champion.

items.action: Run a competitive-positioning workshop internally (deal team + deal owner's manager + product/Merlin expert) within 14 days to define the Merlin Intake wedge against Coupa, GEP, and JAGGAER for this specific deal. The workshop must answer: (1) Which of the three is most likely the incumbent at UNH, and what is our displacement strategy? (2) What is the Merlin Intake best-of-breed wedge vs their bundled P2P suites? (3) How do we position on AI differentiation, integration simplicity, and total cost of ownership? (4) What is our 'how we win' one-liner for each competitor? (5) What discovery questions will reveal the incumbent and the buyer's bundle-vs-best-of-breed philosophy? Arm the rep with a tight competitive narrative before the discovery call so we can activate the wedge in real time.
items.trigger: Three major competitors logged (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER) on deal creation (2026-06-25), no discovery of incumbent or competitive context, no positioning conversation, zero Avoma calls. We are blind going into the discovery call.
items.expected_effect: Competitive-positioning workshop ensures we enter the discovery call with a sharp wedge and the right questions to uncover the incumbent and evaluation philosophy. Without this prep, we risk being reactive (answering RFP questions) instead of proactive (shaping the criteria around our strengths). Expected effect: tighten the Merlin Intake AI-native narrative, define the wedge vs each competitor, and increase win probability by positioning early and shaping the evaluation.

items.action: Executive connect: the deal owner's manager (named in ground-truth authoritative facts as Michael McCarthy) to reach out to a VP-level or C-level executive at UNH (VP Finance, CIO, Provost) within 21 days if the discovery call reveals a strategic AI or procurement-transformation initiative. This is a forcing function to elevate the conversation beyond procurement and to signal that Zycus takes this deal seriously. The manager outreach should be framed as a value-add: 'I lead our higher-ed practice and wanted to personally ensure we are aligned on your procurement modernization goals. Can we schedule 20 minutes to discuss how Merlin Intake fits into your broader digital strategy?' This move is conditional on the discovery call confirming a real opportunity (not an RFP trap).
items.trigger: No EB or executive sponsor identified (SF X10a_Sponsor__c, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c unread/null, OpportunityContactRole shows only Jesse Frink, procurement coordinator). 75K public-sector deal requires VP/C-level sign-off.
items.expected_effect: Manager-to-executive outreach accomplishes three things: (1) identifies and engages the Economic Buyer, (2) elevates the conversation to strategic level (procurement transformation, AI adoption, digital modernization), (3) signals executive commitment from Zycus and differentiates us from competitors who may be single-threading through procurement. Expected effect: map the EB, establish executive sponsorship, and increase win probability by creating a peer relationship at the top of the org chart. Conditional on discovery call validating the opportunity.

items.action: Validate qualification criteria and consider disqualifying if the discovery call reveals an unwinnable RFP trap. The deal was marked Qualified on creation (2026-06-25) with no discovery, which is a process shortcut. After the discovery call, assess: (1) Is there a real pain and business case, or is this a paper exercise? (2) Do we have access to a champion, coach, or business owner, or are we locked into procurement? (3) Is an incumbent competitor entrenched, and do we have a wedge to displace them? (4) Are we positioned to shape the evaluation, or are we responding to a locked RFP? If the answers are negative (RFP trap, no champion, incumbent advantage, no wedge), disqualify the deal and reallocate time to higher-probability opportunities. Disqualifying fast is a win, not a loss.
items.trigger: Deal marked Qualified on creation day (2026-06-25) with no discovery evidence, three major competitors, no champion, no EB, no buyer engagement. High risk of RFP courtesy inclusion.
items.expected_effect: Honest qualification assessment ensures we do not waste cycles on an unwinnable deal. If the discovery call confirms we are an RFP box-check (no champion, no access, incumbent advantage, locked criteria), disqualifying immediately frees the rep to focus on deals with real win probability. If the call confirms a real opportunity (pain, access, wedge), we double down. Expected effect: improve forecast accuracy, protect rep capacity, and avoid the trap of 'hopium' (keeping a dead deal in the pipe because we want it to be real).

confidence_signals.summary: This deal exhibits zero confidence signals and multiple red flags. No buyer engagement (no discovery call, no Salesforce activities, no Avoma conversations), single-threaded through a procurement coordinator with no champion or EB access, qualified without discovery, and competing against three major vendors with no positioning. The deal is five days old and entirely dark. These are not the signals of a deal in motion; they are the signals of a deal that may never have been real or that we joined too late and too weak to compete. Confidence is absent.

north_star_verdict.math: Days to close: 232 (close 2027-02-17). Time in Qualified stage: 5 days (created and qualified 2026-06-25). No stage progression. No submission-date series on record (Qualified_Submission_Date__c, Formal_Eval_Submission_Date__c, Shortlisted_Submission_Date__c all unread or null). No field history of stage moves or amount/close-date changes in the five-day window. LastActivityDate is null, meaning zero logged Salesforce activities (tasks, events, emails) since creation. This is not a stall; this is a deal that never started. For a 232-day cycle, the required pace is discovery and multi-threading within the first 30 days, RFI/RFP positioning by day 60, shortlisting by day 120, and commercials by day 180. We are on day 5 with no discovery call scheduled and no buyer engagement, which would be recoverable if we had a champion or inbound interest, but we have neither. The deal is off pace before it began.
evidence: Created 2026-06-25, qualified same day with no evidence of discovery or buyer dialogue (SF CreatedDate, Qualified stage with no preceding Prospecting/Discovery moves)

Zero Salesforce activities: LastActivityDate is null, no tasks, no events, no logged emails in the five-day window (SOQL OpportunityFieldHistory, Task, Event queries returned no buyer-touching records)
Single contact role: Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing, Evaluator role, no interaction date, no champion/EB/DM roles identified (SF OpportunityContactRole)
Three competitors logged on creation (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, JAGGAER) with no context, discovery call, or competitive-positioning conversation (SF Competitors__c)

Next step is rep-side send with no buyer commitment: 'Justin to send showpad material in preparation for a disco call and eventual walkthrough of merlin intake' but no scheduled call, no buyer reply, no meeting invite (SF Next_Step__c)

Amount 75K is within public-sector point-solution range but we have no discovery of budget authority, procurement timeline, RFP scope, or decision process (no discovery call, no fields populated beyond stage/amount/competitors/products)
north_star_verdict.headline: Dead in the water: five-day-old opportunity created 2026-06-25, no buyer engagement ever recorded, no discovery call, no contact beyond a single OpportunityContactRole (Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing), and zero Salesforce activities. The deal was marked Qualified on creation without any evidence of two-way dialogue. With three major competitors logged (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER) and a 232-day runway to a Feb 2027 close, this deal exhibits all the signatures of an RFP courtesy inclusion: we were added to satisfy a multi-vendor requirement, not because we have a champion or a wedge. The only recorded next step is rep-side (Justin to send Showpad material), with no buyer reply or scheduled discovery call. In a public-sector procurement this cold, we are running blind into a price-driven RFP with incumbents who have integration and training advantages we have not mapped. Without a live buyer conversation in the next 7 days, this deal should be disqualified or moved to a watch list.

flags: No buyer engagement: Zero discovery calls, zero Salesforce activities (LastActivityDate null), zero Avoma calls discovered in opp+account+attendee search (usnh.edu, Jesse Frink). Deal is five days old and entirely dark. Rep has offered to send Showpad material but no call is scheduled and no buyer reply recorded. Without a live conversation in the next 7 days, this deal should be disqualified or moved to Omitted.

Single-threaded through procurement coordinator: Only one contact (Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing, Evaluator role). No champion, no EB, no DM, no business owner, no budget authority. Jesse's title suggests he is running a process, not advocating for Zycus. In a competitive deal with three major vendors, single-threading through procurement is a recipe for losing on price. We must multi-thread to the pain owner and budget owner in the first two weeks or disqualify.

Qualified without discovery: Deal was created and marked Qualified on the same day (2026-06-25) with no evidence of buyer dialogue, discovery call, or need confirmation. This suggests a process shortcut (rep qualified speculatively based on RFP invite or partner referral) or a data-quality issue (deal was imported or auto-created by an integration). A deal should not be Qualified until discovery confirms the pain, the budget, the timeline, and the decision process. We are running blind. The immediate move is to validate qualification criteria with the deal owner and either run discovery or disqualify.

Three major competitors, no positioning: Coupa, GEP Worldwide, and JAGGAER are logged as of deal creation, suggesting a known multi-vendor evaluation. We have not yet engaged the buyer, so we have not discovered the incumbent, the evaluation criteria, or our differentiation wedge. We are at risk of being the 'AI vendor' in a price-driven RFP where the buyer defaults to the bundled suite or the safe incumbent. The discovery call must extract the competitive context and activate the Merlin Intake wedge within the first 14 days.

No champion or coach identified: Jesse Frink is an Evaluator, not a Champion. We have no evidence of advocacy, no internal ally, no coach. In a competitive RFP scenario, a deal with no champion is a deal we are not positioned to win. The discovery call must assess Jesse's stance and secure introductions to a business owner or pain owner who can advocate internally. If Jesse is unwilling to open doors, we must find another entry point or disqualify.

No Economic Buyer or budget authority identified: Jesse Frink is a procurement coordinator, not a budget owner. In a 75K public-university deal, approval will require a VP Finance, CFO, or Provost-level sponsor. We have not identified or engaged that person. Without EB access, we cannot confirm budget, political support, or organizational priority. The discovery call must map the org chart and identify the budget owner.
best_practice_check.summary: This deal violates multiple enterprise sales fundamentals and is at high risk of being an unwinnable RFP courtesy inclusion. The critical gaps: (1) No buyer engagement: zero discovery calls, zero Salesforce activities, zero Avoma conversations in the five days since creation. (2) Single-threaded: one contact (Jesse Frink, procurement coordinator) with no champion, no EB, no DM, no multi-threading to the business owner or budget authority. (3) Qualified without discovery: the deal was marked Qualified on creation (2026-06-25) with no evidence of need confirmation, pain identification, or buyer dialogue, suggesting a process shortcut or data-quality issue. (4) Three major competitors logged with no positioning: Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER are formidable, and we have not yet engaged the buyer to discover the incumbent, the evaluation criteria, or our wedge. (5) No champion or coach: Jesse is an Evaluator, not an advocate, and we have no internal ally. (6) No EB or budget authority mapped: in a 75K public-sector deal, we must identify and engage the CFO, VP Finance, or Provost-level sponsor, and we have not. (7) Long runway with no plan: 232 days to close, but no discovery call scheduled, no multi-threading plan, no competitive-positioning strategy. These gaps are individually serious and collectively fatal. The deal should not have been marked Qualified, and it should not remain in the forecast without immediate re-engagement.

competitive_position.summary: We are the underdog in a three-way fight against Coupa, GEP Worldwide, and JAGGAER. All three are logged in Salesforce as of deal creation (2026-06-25), suggesting this is a known multi-vendor evaluation, likely an RFP or informal competitive tender. We have not yet engaged the buyer, so we have not positioned Merlin Intake, discovered the incumbent, or learned what matters most to UNH in this evaluation. Our competitive position is weak by default: no discovery, no champion, no differentiation conversation, and no knowledge of whether we are fighting an incumbent or a clean-sheet procurement. The wedge exists (Merlin's AI-native intake vs the competitors' legacy P2P front-ends) but it is unactivated. We are at risk of being the 'AI vendor' in a price-driven RFP where the buyer defaults to the bundled suite or the incumbent.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin Intake is an AI-native product (intelligent requisition orchestration, agentic automation, ML-powered triage and routing), so the AI positioning opportunity is structural. But we have not yet engaged the buyer, so the AI narrative is unactivated. We do not know if UNH values AI capability (a board-level priority, a faculty-facing differentiator) or views it as a nice-to-have. We do not know if they are aware of Merlin's AI differentiation vs the competitors' legacy P2P intake layers. The AI positioning strength is latent, not realized. The discovery call must extract their AI appetite and establish Merlin Intake as the AI-first choice in this category. If AI matters to them, it is our wedge; if it does not, we must pivot to integration simplicity, speed to value, or cost.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: We cannot assess fit because we have not yet discovered the customer's expectations. The product scope (Merlin Intake) is defined, and we know the competitors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER), but we do not know UNH's priorities (cost vs capability, AI vs integration, best-of-breed vs bundled, speed vs features), their evaluation criteria, their current-state pain, or their decision process. Fit is unknown. The discovery call is the fit diagnostic.

hard.ais_why:
• USNH is 'exploring AI capabilities for supplier risk visibility and procurement intake,' with the compelling event framed as 'Exploring AI capabilities to enhance procurement.'
• Merlin Intake is scoped and the buyer currently runs Jaggaer and Workday, positioning Zycus AI as an additive layer.
• The Agentic-platform-discussed flag is not set and the step is sending Showpad material for a discovery call, so AI interest is genuine but still early.
hard.next_step: Justin to send showpad material in preparation for a disco call and eventual walkthrough of merlin intake

value.name: Jesse Frink
value.risk: Single point of contact with no evidence of engagement or advocacy. Title suggests procurement coordinator role (running a process, not a pain owner or budget owner). Evaluator role in OpportunityContactRole (not Champion, not EB, not DM) confirms he is not the decision-maker. We have not yet spoken to Jesse, so we do not know his stance (neutral vs supportive), his access to the EB/DM, or his willingness to open doors. Risk: if Jesse is simply administering an RFP and we cannot get introductions to the business owner and budget authority, we are locked into a single-threaded, price-driven process with no champion and no wedge. The discovery call must assess Jesse's stance and secure multi-threading.
value.title: Director of Contracts and Sourcing
value.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Jesse Frink, Director of Contracts and Sourcing, jesse.frink@usnh.edu, Evaluator), zero Avoma calls, zero tasks/events showing interaction

value.name: Jesse Frink
value.risk: Single point of contact with no evidence of advocacy or access to Economic Buyer. Procurement coordinator role suggests limited influence on business decision. No engagement since deal creation.
value.title: Director of Contracts and Sourcing

value.name: Coupa
value.source: SF Competitors__c, logged at deal creation
value.how_we_win: Merlin Intake as a best-of-breed AI orchestration layer that integrates with Coupa or replaces Coupa's intake front-end with a modern, agentic user experience. Positioning: 'Keep your Coupa back-end, upgrade your intake intelligence.' We must discover if Coupa is the incumbent; if so, this is a displacement play and we need a pain-based wedge (poor UX, slow triage, no AI). If Coupa is a competitor but not incumbent, we position on AI differentiation and cost (Merlin Intake is cheaper than a full Coupa rip-and-replace). Activate this wedge in the discovery call by asking what they like and dislike about their current intake process and whether they are evaluating Coupa as a full-suite replacement or an intake-only solution.

value.name: Coupa
value.how_we_win: Position Merlin Intake's agentic AI and intelligent requisition automation as a quantum leap beyond Coupa's rules-based intake workflows; emphasize that Merlin learns from historical patterns and negotiates intake bottlenecks autonomously. Make AI capability a top-weighted criterion in the RFP and demonstrate live with UNH's data in the discovery call.

value.name: GEP Worldwide
value.source: SF Competitors__c, logged at deal creation
value.how_we_win: GEP is services-heavy and enterprise-complex. We position Merlin Intake as faster to deploy, lower total cost of ownership, and purpose-built for intake intelligence (not a generalist S2P suite). Wedge: 'GEP will take 12-18 months and a big services engagement to configure and deploy. Merlin Intake is 90 days to value, SaaS-native, and built for the AI-first era.' We must discover if GEP is being considered for full S2P or intake-only; if full S2P, we may lose on breadth unless we can prove intake is the critical bottleneck and the rest of their stack is fine. Activate this wedge by asking about their implementation timeline, appetite for a big services-led project, and whether they are looking to replace their entire P2P stack or solve a specific intake pain.

value.name: JAGGAER
value.source: SF Competitors__c, logged at deal creation
value.how_we_win: JAGGAER (formerly SciQuest) has deep higher-ed roots and strong university references, which is a threat in a university deal. But JAGGAER's intake layer is legacy (built for indirect procurement, not AI-powered orchestration), and their platform is aging compared to modern cloud-native solutions. We position Merlin Intake as the AI-first, next-generation intake experience that universities are moving toward, with intelligent routing, agentic automation, and a user experience that faculty and staff will actually use (vs the clunky JAGGAER forms). Wedge: 'JAGGAER is the safe, familiar choice, but it is 2015 technology. Merlin Intake is what procurement looks like in 2026: AI-powered, mobile-friendly, and built for the hybrid work era.' We must discover if JAGGAER is the incumbent; if so, we are fighting inertia and references, and we need a pain-based wedge (user complaints, slow adoption, lack of AI). Activate this wedge by asking what their current intake system is, how users feel about it, and whether they are looking for incremental improvement or a generational leap.

value.detail: RFP trap risk: Deal was qualified without buyer engagement, suggesting we were added to a multi-vendor RFP as a courtesy or compliance inclusion. With three major competitors (Coupa, GEP, JAGGAER), no champion, no EB relationship, and no discovery call, we may be fulfilling a 'three-bid requirement' with no real chance to win. The next 14 days will determine if this is a genuine opportunity or a paper exercise. If we cannot secure a discovery call, multi-thread to the business owner, and identify a champion or coach, we should disqualify rather than waste cycles on an unwinnable RFP.

value.detail: Public-sector pricing pressure: Universities are highly price-sensitive and often select the lowest-cost qualified bidder in RFP processes. Coupa, GEP, and JAGGAER all offer bundled P2P suites that may appear more cost-effective than a point-solution Merlin Intake license (75K for intake-only vs a full-suite contract that includes intake, sourcing, contracts, procurement, and invoicing). We have not yet discovered the budget, procurement constraints, or pricing expectations. Risk that 75K amount is aspirational or that the buyer will negotiate down aggressively. We must establish the business case and quantified ROI early (discovery call) and position Merlin Intake as lower total cost of ownership than a full-suite rip-and-replace, not just lower unit price.

value.detail: Incumbent advantage: If any of the three competitors (especially Coupa or JAGGAER) is already deployed at UNH or within the University System of New Hampshire for other procurement modules (sourcing, contracts, invoicing, expenses), they will have a massive incumbent advantage due to integration, training, vendor relationship inertia, and organizational familiarity. We do not know the current-state tech stack (ERP: Workday, Oracle, PeopleSoft? P2P: Coupa, JAGGAER, Ariba, GEP, homegrown?) or whether any competitor is the incumbent. This is a critical discovery question. If an incumbent exists, we are fighting inertia and references, and our wedge must be pain-based (their intake layer is broken, users hate it, it is holding them back) plus AI differentiation. If no incumbent, we have a cleaner fight, but we must still prove integration simplicity.

value.detail: No executive sponsor or budget authority identified: In a 75K public-sector deal, approval will require a VP-level or C-level sponsor (VP Finance, CFO, Provost, CIO). We have one contact (Jesse Frink, procurement coordinator) with no access to or engagement with the budget owner. Without identifying and engaging the Economic Buyer, we cannot confirm budget availability, political support, or organizational priority for this initiative. Single-threading through procurement is a recipe for stalling at the commercials stage. The discovery call must map the org chart and secure an introduction to the budget owner within the first 30 days.
